question: \text { 5. Show that the integer next above }(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 n} \text { is divisible by } 2^{n+1} \text {. }
solution:
Solution. The key to this and many similar problems is that the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 n} \) is \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 n}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 n} \). To establish this, note that for every positive integer \( n \), there are integers \( A_{n} \) and \( B_{n} \) such that
\[
(1+\sqrt{3})^{2 n}=A_{n}+B_{n} \sqrt{3}
\]
and
\[
(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 n}=A_{n}-B_{n} \sqrt{3}
\]

Thus \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 n}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 n}=2 A_{n} \) is certainly an integer. Since \( |1-\sqrt{3}|<1 \), we have \( 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 n}<1 \). Hence \( 2 A_{n} \) is indeed the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 n} \).

The problem, therefore, is to show that \( 2 A_{n} \) is divisible by \( 2^{n+1} \), or that \( A_{n} \) is divisible by \( 2^{n} \).

We claim that for all \( n \), both \( A_{n} \) and \( B_{n} \) are divisible by \( 2^{n} \). We prove this by induction. Since \( A_{1}=4 \) and \( B_{1}=2 \), it is true for \( n=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( n=k \). Then we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_{k+1}+B_{k+1} \sqrt{3} & =(1+\sqrt{3})^{2}\left(A_{k}+B_{k} \sqrt{3}\right) \\
& =\left(4 A_{k}+6 B_{k}\right)+\left(2 A_{k}+4 B_{k}\right) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_{k+1} & =2\left(2 A_{k}+3 B_{k}\right), \\
B_{k+1} & =2\left(A_{k}+2 B_{k}\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
and it is clear from our inductive hypothesis that both of these are divisible by \( 2^{k+1} \). This establishes our claim.

descriptive_long.question: \text { 5. Show that the integer next above }(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 indexn} \text { is divisible by } 2^{indexn+1} \text {. }
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The key to this and many similar problems is that the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn} \) is \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn} \). To establish this, note that for every positive integer \( indexn \), there are integers \( coeffan \) and \( coeffbn \) such that
\[
(1+\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}=coeffan+coeffbn \sqrt{3}
\]
and
\[
(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}=coeffan-coeffbn \sqrt{3}
\]

Thus \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}=2 coeffan \) is certainly an integer. Since \( |1-\sqrt{3}|<1 \), we have \( 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn}<1 \). Hence \( 2 coeffan \) is indeed the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 indexn} \).

The problem, therefore, is to show that \( 2 coeffan \) is divisible by \( 2^{indexn+1} \), or that \( coeffan \) is divisible by \( 2^{indexn} \).

We claim that for all \( indexn \), both \( coeffan \) and \( coeffbn \) are divisible by \( 2^{indexn} \). We prove this by induction. Since \( coeffaone =4 \) and \( coeffbone =2 \), it is true for \( indexn =1 \). Suppose it is true for \( indexn =iterat \). Then we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
coeffasuc+coeffbsuc \sqrt{3} & =(1+\sqrt{3})^{2}\left(coeffak+coeffbk \sqrt{3}\right) \\
& =\left(4 coeffak+6 coeffbk\right)+\left(2 coeffak+4 coeffbk\right) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
coeffasuc & =2\left(2 coeffak+3 coeffbk\right), \\
coeffbsuc & =2\left(coeffak+2 coeffbk\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
and it is clear from our inductive hypothesis that both of these are divisible by \( 2^{iterat+1} \). This establishes our claim.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: \text { 5. Show that the integer next above }(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 sunflower} \text { is divisible by } 2^{sunflower+1} \text {. }
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The key to this and many similar problems is that the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower} \) is \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower} \). To establish this, note that for every positive integer \( sunflower \), there are integers \( teaspoon \) and \( raincloud \) such that
\[
(1+\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}=teaspoon+raincloud \sqrt{3}
\]
and
\[
(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}=teaspoon-raincloud \sqrt{3}
\]

Thus \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}=2 teaspoon \) is certainly an integer. Since \( |1-\sqrt{3}|<1 \), we have \( 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower}<1 \). Hence \( 2 teaspoon \) is indeed the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 sunflower} \).

The problem, therefore, is to show that \( 2 teaspoon \) is divisible by \( 2^{sunflower+1} \), or that \( teaspoon \) is divisible by \( 2^{sunflower} \).

We claim that for all \( sunflower \), both \( teaspoon \) and \( raincloud \) are divisible by \( 2^{sunflower} \). We prove this by induction. Since \( firebrick=4 \) and \( scarecrow=2 \), it is true for \( sunflower=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( sunflower=parchment \). Then we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
goldsmith+lighthouse \sqrt{3} & =(1+\sqrt{3})^{2}\left(sandpaper+toothpick \sqrt{3}\right) \\
& =\left(4 sandpaper+6 toothpick\right)+\left(2 sandpaper+4 toothpick\right) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
goldsmith & =2\left(2 sandpaper+3 toothpick\right), \\
lighthouse & =2\left(sandpaper+2 toothpick\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
and it is clear from our inductive hypothesis that both of these are divisible by \( 2^{parchment+1} \). This establishes our claim.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: \text { 5. Show that the integer next above }(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 infinitecount} \text { is divisible by } 2^{infinitecount+1} \text {. }
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The key to this and many similar problems is that the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount} \) is \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount} \). To establish this, note that for every positive integer \( infinitecount \), there are integers \( nonintegralseq \) and \( irrationalseq \) such that
\[
(1+\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}=nonintegralseq+irrationalseq \sqrt{3}
\]
and
\[
(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}=nonintegralseq-irrationalseq \sqrt{3}
\]

Thus \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}=2 nonintegralseq \) is certainly an integer. Since \( |1-\sqrt{3}|<1 \), we have \( 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount}<1 \). Hence \( 2 nonintegralseq \) is indeed the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 infinitecount} \).

The problem, therefore, is to show that \( 2 nonintegralseq \) is divisible by \( 2^{infinitecount+1} \), or that \( nonintegralseq \) is divisible by \( 2^{infinitecount} \).

We claim that for all \( infinitecount \), both \( nonintegralseq \) and \( irrationalseq \) are divisible by \( 2^{infinitecount} \). We prove this by induction. Since \( nonintegralone=4 \) and \( irrationalone=2 \), it is true for \( infinitecount=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( infinitecount=constantterm \). Then we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
nonintegralsucc+irrationalsucc \sqrt{3} & =(1+\sqrt{3})^{2}\left(nonintegralkey+irrationalkey \sqrt{3}\right) \\
& =\left(4 nonintegralkey+6 irrationalkey\right)+\left(2 nonintegralkey+4 irrationalkey\right) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
nonintegralsucc & =2\left(2 nonintegralkey+3 irrationalkey\right), \\
irrationalsucc & =2\left(nonintegralkey+2 irrationalkey\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
and it is clear from our inductive hypothesis that both of these are divisible by \( 2^{constantterm+1} \). This establishes our claim.

garbled_string.question: \text { 5. Show that the integer next above }(\sqrt{3}+1)^{2 qzxwvtnp} \text { is divisible by } 2^{qzxwvtnp+1} \text {. }
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The key to this and many similar problems is that the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp} \) is \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp} \). To establish this, note that for every positive integer \( qzxwvtnp \), there are integers \( mnrpsfql \) and \( gklvzrtu \) such that
\[
(1+\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}=mnrpsfql+gklvzrtu \sqrt{3}
\]
and
\[
(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}=mnrpsfql-gklvzrtu \sqrt{3}
\]

Thus \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}=2 mnrpsfql \) is certainly an integer. Since \( |1-\sqrt{3}|<1 \), we have \( 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp}<1 \). Hence \( 2 mnrpsfql \) is indeed the next integer above \( (1+\sqrt{3})^{2 qzxwvtnp} \).

The problem, therefore, is to show that \( 2 mnrpsfql \) is divisible by \( 2^{qzxwvtnp+1} \), or that \( mnrpsfql \) is divisible by \( 2^{qzxwvtnp} \).

We claim that for all \( qzxwvtnp \), both \( mnrpsfql \) and \( gklvzrtu \) are divisible by \( 2^{qzxwvtnp} \). We prove this by induction. Since \( pskdhqwe=4 \) and \( xnvbtyra=2 \), it is true for \( qzxwvtnp=1 \). Suppose it is true for \( qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla \). Then we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
dqwpmzci+zyvfwots \sqrt{3} & =(1+\sqrt{3})^{2}\left(lqzmxnwe+chyrbtkl \sqrt{3}\right) \\
& =\left(4 lqzmxnwe+6 chyrbtkl\right)+\left(2 lqzmxnwe+4 chyrbtkl\right) \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
\]
so
\[
\begin{aligned}
dqwpmzci & =2\left(2 lqzmxnwe+3 chyrbtkl\right), \\
zyvfwots & =2\left(lqzmxnwe+2 chyrbtkl\right),
\end{aligned}
\]
and it is clear from our inductive hypothesis that both of these are divisible by \( 2^{hjgrksla+1} \). This establishes our claim.

kernel_variant.question: For every non-negative integer $n$, let\[M_n=\Bigl(1+\sqrt{3}\Bigr)^{4n}.\]Denote by $I_n$ the least integer that is strictly larger than $M_n$.  Prove that\[2^{2n+1}\mid I_n\quad\text{for every }n\ge 0.\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Let M_n=(1+\sqrt{3})^{4n} (n\geq 0),    I_n=\lceil M_n\rceil  (the least integer >M_n).  We prove 2^{2n+1}\mid I_n.  

1. The ``next-integer'' expression.  For every integer m\geq 1 we have |1-\sqrt{3}|<1, hence 0<(1-\sqrt{3})^{2m}<1.  Therefore
    J_m:=(1+\sqrt{3})^{2m}+(1-\sqrt{3})^{2m}
is an integer that lies strictly between (1+\sqrt{3})^{2m} and (1+\sqrt{3})^{2m}+1; i.e.
    J_m
is the next integer above (1+\sqrt{3})^{2m}.  For m=0 we obtain J_0=2, which is indeed the next integer above (1+\sqrt{3})^0=1,
so the statement also holds when m=0.

2. Reduction to the required case.  Put m=2n.  Then
    I_n = J_{2n} = (1+\sqrt{3})^{4n} + (1-\sqrt{3})^{4n}.
Consequently it suffices to prove that J_m is divisible by 2^{m+1} for every m\geq 0.

3. Separating rational and irrational parts.  Write
    (1+\sqrt{3})^{2m} = C_m + D_m\sqrt{3},  C_m,D_m\in \mathbb{Z}.
Conjugating \sqrt{3} gives
    (1-\sqrt{3})^{2m} = C_m - D_m\sqrt{3},
whence
    J_m = 2C_m.
Our task is thus to show
    2^m \mid  C_m  (m\geq 0).

4. A doubling recursion.  Because (1+\sqrt{3})^2 = 4+2\sqrt{3} = 2(2+\sqrt{3}), we have
    (1+\sqrt{3})^{2(m+1)}
      = (4+2\sqrt{3})(C_m + D_m\sqrt{3})
      = (4C_m+6D_m) + (2C_m+4D_m)\sqrt{3.}
Hence
    C_{m+1} = 4C_m + 6D_m = 2(2C_m + 3D_m),
    D_{m+1} = 2C_m + 4D_m = 2(C_m + 2D_m).
Both C_{m+1} and D_{m+1} carry an overall factor 2.

5. Induction.  Base case m=0: (1+\sqrt{3})^0=1 gives C_0=1, D_0=0; clearly 1=2^0 divides both numbers.

Inductive step: assume 2^m \mid  C_m and 2^m \mid  D_m.  Then
    C_{m+1} = 2(2C_m+3D_m),
    D_{m+1} = 2(C_m+2D_m),
and since 2^m divides both C_m and D_m, it divides 2C_m+3D_m and C_m+2D_m, hence 2^{m+1} divides C_{m+1}, D_{m+1}.

Consequently J_m = 2C_m is divisible by 2\cdot 2^m = 2^{m+1}.

6. Conclusion for the original problem.  Taking m=2n gives
    I_n = J_{2n} \Rightarrow  2^{2n+1} \mid  I_n.

Therefore the integer immediately following (1+\sqrt{3})^{4n} is always a multiple of 2^{2n+1}. \blacksquare

core_steps: Size bound: 0 < (1−√3)^{2n} < 1 so 2A_n is the ceiling of (1+√3)^{2n}
Define integer sequences A_n, B_n via (1+√3)^{2n} = A_n + B_n√3
Recurrence: (1+√3)^2(A_k + B_k√3) gives A_{k+1}, B_{k+1} each containing an overall factor 2
Induction on n: base case then recurrence ⇒ A_n divisible by 2^n ⇒ 2A_n divisible by 2^{n+1}

slot1.description: Names of the integer sequences arising from the expansion

slot2.description: Choice of the initial index for the induction (could start at n=0 instead of n=1)
